narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.60, an LSAT score of 38, and your rankings in the lower deciles for your Year 1 and Year 3 grades at school—specifically the 3rd decile early on and the 1st decile by Year 3—along with coming from a lower-middle family income bracket and planning to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated your probability of failing at 58.9%. This tipped the scales just over the threshold, leading to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed on the bar right away. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you at the bottom of your class that year—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average—and signaled to the model a potential struggle with sustained high performance. Next in importance was your LSAT score, which actually worked in your favor by exceeding the average of 36.7, pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing and showing your aptitude for legal reasoning. Your Year 1 decile ranking also contributed toward failure, though less strongly, as being in the 3rd decile there was noticeably below the average 5.71, hinting at early academic challenges. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile stands out for those weaker decile positions despite the solid LSAT, which is common for successful applicants around your age and background. While your full-time status aligns with most students and your family income is in line with many Hispanic applicants who succeed, the model weighed your grade trajectory most heavily. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer—many overcome similar starts through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further or enrolling in a post-baccalaureate program to improve your GPA and decile rankings, then reapply with that stronger academic story. Building real-world legal experience, like internships, could also help demonstrate resilience. You're clearly driven, and with focused steps on those key academic areas, law school doors can reopen for you.
